sample_0001: Granite timestamps are ok until the third segment, where they pick up a ~3s offset after a ~7s silence. Speaker attribution identifies two speakers when there are three (two share a channel, but that isn't how Granite assigns them).
sample_0002: Granite timestamps lag from the third segment, with the lag increasing until it repeats a phrase around ~116s (the only time I've noticed it hallucinate), and by doing so, strangely corrects its timing, then starts lagging again. Speaker assignment is inconsistent.
sample_0003: Granite doesn't hallucinate at the end like WhisperX does, and its timestamps are actually good right up until the last segment after a long silence, but it misses a full repeated phrase at ~179s.
sample_0004: Here Granite's timestamps actually lead instead of lag after second segment. It also misses an interrupting speaker at ~44s, but as WhisperX gets split stereo channels, it isn't a fair comparison.
sample_0005: Neither model handle the interruptions in this example around 30s. Timestamps are ok for a full ~200s, but then they start to lag pretty badly.
sample_0006: Here's a nice example of WhipserX panicking during long silences and hallucinating in multiple languages. Granite handles the silence much better, transcripting accurately, but the timestamps are completely off.
sample_0007: Interrupting speakers get attributed to the same single-speaker segment by Granite, and the timestamps lag after the third segment.
sample_0008: WhisperX hallucinates all over the show, Granite does much better on transcribing but the timestamps lag badly.
sample_0009: An example that has a whole lot of silence. Interestingly WhisperX doesn't go to pieces as much on this one, but Granite doesn't hallucinate at all. Unfortunately it puts both speakers into a single segment and its timestamps are off.
sample_0010: In this real recording, Granite lags a little but not by as much as the synthetic examples. It merges speakers into the same single-speaker segment. In this example WhisperX also does a better job on word accuracy.
sample_0011: I added this example because I suspected that a long single-speaker example without silences might resolve Granite's timestamp issues. It does seem to, and here the transcript is pretty much identically good to WhisperX (it even transcribed my double-take on a misread word), it's just a pity you don't get punctuation or capitalization. I do wonder if both models were happy to be given such a canonical text to transcribe?
